summary: When verifying your own prior claim (Slack reply, ADR, assertion), dispatch an adversarial reviewer AND an evaluator in parallel. One lens alone misses either the conceptual or the methodology error class.
root_cause: A single reviewer focuses either on conceptual soundness OR methodology — not both. Self-verification fails because the verifier shares the same frame as the original claimant.
fix: Dispatch socrates-contrarian (concept attack) and evaluator (methodology check) in parallel. Wait for both receipts before accepting the claim as verified.

summary: ArgoCD access requires three planes: AAD group membership + Enterprise App assignment (groupMembershipClaims:ApplicationGroup) + AppProject role binding. Any missing plane = silent denial.
root_cause: ArgoCD RBAC has three independent permission planes. AAD membership alone is insufficient. The Enterprise App must have groupMembershipClaims:ApplicationGroup set, AND the AppProject must grant the matching role. Missing any one produces silent denial, not an error.
fix: Check all three planes in sequence: (1) az ad group member check; (2) verify Enterprise App groupMembershipClaims setting; (3) kubectl get appproject and check roleBindings. Fix whichever plane is missing.

summary: ADO stage with trigger:none + approval gate that times out → stage silently skips. The 2-hour-exact delta in Timeline Checkpoint.Approval records distinguishes timeout (ADO default) from 'plan no change'. Read Timeline records, not just pipeline logs.
root_cause: ADO's default approval timeout is 2 hours. When trigger:none prevents automatic queuing and the approval window expires, the stage is marked skipped — not failed. Pipeline logs show 'skipped', not 'timed out', creating ambiguity.
fix: Read the build Timeline API records and check Checkpoint.Approval delta. 2h-exact delta = ADO default approval timeout. Distinguish from plan-no-change skip by checking if Terraform ran at all.

summary: Rootly resolve does NOT close upstream Azure Monitor alerts. RCA action steps must explicitly drive both Rootly and Microsoft.AlertsManagement/alerts/<id> to terminal state. Resolving only one leaves a zombie alert.
root_cause: Rootly and Azure Monitor alerts are independent systems. Rootly resolve is an incident management action; it does not call the Azure API to fire off alert resolution. Azure Monitor alerts remain active until explicitly resolved via az monitor or REST API.
fix: After resolving in Rootly, also run: az rest --method POST --url '/subscriptions/{sub}/providers/Microsoft.AlertsManagement/alerts/{id}/changestate?api-version=2019-05-05-preview&newState=Closed'. Confirm both planes show terminal state.

summary: CCoE Key Vault module hardcodes ServiceApiLatency Avg>1000ms alert — fires on low-volume bootstrap KVs ~every 2-3.5 weeks across all products. This alert class is structurally noisy, not operationally actionable.
root_cause: CCoE baseline module has a hardcoded alert threshold that is not tuned for bootstrap/low-volume Key Vaults. Bootstrap KVs have sporadic request patterns that naturally exceed the latency threshold.
fix: On receipt of CCoE KeyVault latency alert on a bootstrap KV: verify it is a bootstrap KV (low traffic expected), verify no active operations in flight, close as expected-noise. Do not escalate or page unless concurrent with a real incident.

summary: Credential expiry is a recurring CLASS problem at Trade Platform (5 incidents in 18 months: INC-75 AAD SP 2024-11-19; F4 AAD SP 2025-12-29; PXQ KV 2026-05-07; argo-cd-sandbox PAT 2026-05-11; latent 2026-06-01 3 MC PATs). Per-incident rotation does NOT reduce class recurrence. Class-level remediation mandatory: eliminate calendar-expiring credentials (Workload Identity Federation) OR automate rotation (KV + ESO + scheduler). Standing rule: any PR introducing a new credential MUST declare rotation owner, verification path, alarm surface in the same PR.
root_cause: Manual oral procedure carried by one engineer (Fabrizio Zavalloni); no class-level structural fix has shipped despite multiple post-incident promises since 2024-11 (INC-75). Each instance is firefought as if novel.
fix: Phase 1 (now-30d, unconditional): SLA + Grafana alert (argocd_appset_status{condition_type='ErrorOccurred'} > 0) + named ownership rotation. Phase 2 (30-180d): choose Workload Identity Federation OR KV+ESO scheduled rotation based on Fabrizio's gap-list answers in how-to-rotate.md §7. Phase 3 (180d+): extend to F4 AAD SP, ESP cert, TF SP, BTM, Snyk classes.

summary: Azure scheduled-query-rule property autoMitigate=false is ORTHOGONAL to severity. A sev-2 rule with autoMitigate=false has the same 'stays Fired forever' property as a sev-0; severity changes who-gets-paged-how, not auto-close behavior. Any paging-bound rule with autoMitigate=false MUST have: documented manual-close runbook, action group routing to 24/7 surface, runbook URL in rule description. Sev-0 + autoMitigate=false requires extra reviewer.
root_cause: Conflating severity and autoMitigate at design time leads to over-broad sev-0 rules that fire indefinitely on transient triggers (today: CMC vpp-resource-unhealthy fired on Microsoft 5Z1B-6KG late-ingestion).
fix: Add autoMitigate audit to alert-authoring checklist: quarterly query `az monitor scheduled-query list --query "[?autoMitigate==\`false\`].{name:name, sev:severity}"`; each result must have manual-close protocol or justified exception. Block sev-0 + autoMitigate=false in CI gate.

summary: Azure Monitor V2 scheduled-query-rules evaluate windows by ingestion_time(), NOT TimeGenerated. During Microsoft platform latency incidents (5Z1B-6KG: Log Analytics + Application Insights West Europe latency 2026-05-11), workspace rows can land minutes-to-hours after emission. Engine's built-in late-data-settling period delays evaluation; late-arrived rows fall into window and trigger fire. Alerts can fire 20+ min AFTER an event's nominal TimeGenerated. Alert KQL MUST narrow CategoryValue predicates; raw 'ServiceHealth' matches Microsoft's full announcement stream.
root_cause: Out-of-IaC manually-created rule with single-predicate KQL had no narrowing filter; late-ingestion mechanism + Microsoft platform incident combined to fire on stale data.
fix: Pre-deploy adversarial review on any new Azure scheduled-query rule MUST include backtest against a known prior Microsoft platform incident. Prefer 'ResourceHealth + Activated/action + ResourceProviderValue' over raw 'ServiceHealth' for resource-centric semantics. Add ingestion-latency monitoring on workspaces in regions with historical platform incidents (West Europe).

summary: When an incident's RCA must choose between multiple uneliminated causal hypotheses, the correct RCA shape is observation-only — install discriminator probes + reader's mental model; ship NO fix recommendation. Today's CPU throttling RCA (otc-container, eneco-vpp namespace, dev cluster) carried 4 uneliminated hypotheses (H-A undersized CPU, H-B memory pressure upstream, H-C rule mis-calibrated for sidecar class, H-D debug exporter verbose); each implies a different fix. Picking one without elimination ships the wrong fix.
root_cause: Hypothesis dependency is non-trivial (H-A is symptom; H-B/H-D are candidate upstream causes; H-C is orthogonal); a snapshot decision ignores dependency structure.
fix: Document the observation-only L8 template in the RCA process. Adjudication heuristic: run all hypothesis-cheapest-probes; if H-D-class confirms (cheapest+most-reversible), ship that fix first. If H-B-class confirms with H-A-class together, do NOT fix the symptom until the upstream is understood.

summary: At Eneco Trade Platform, on-call incidents land in 4+ different intake channels per shift: #myriad-platform (public, bot-driven cards), #team-platform (private internal triage), Rootly direct page (alertmanager-routed; no Slack mention), ServiceNow CMC ticket (sometimes pasted into Slack as URL, often not). Today's 2026-05-11 shift had FOUR incidents and EACH was in a different channel. Reading only #myriad-platform misses 50%+ of an on-call's actual work. Future summarizers MUST sample ALL surfaces.
root_cause: Multiple alert/intake systems wire to different end-state surfaces with no canonical aggregator. Each surface has its own ownership and notification pattern. Coordination implicit on humans, not on a single dashboard.
fix: On-call summary protocol: enumerate ALL surfaces (Slack public + private + Rootly + SN + RCA dirs) before composing the shift report. Use eneco-context-slack for Slack surfaces, eneco-tools-rootly for Rootly, manual ServiceNow check. Cross-correlate by time window.

summary: FBE slot recycling can leave Azure resources orphaned in rg-vpp-app-sb-401 (Sandbox sub 7b1ba02e-...) that are NOT tracked in the slot's Terraform state. Three uneliminated provenance paths: (P1) failed destroy with terraform state rm workaround; (P2) out-of-band create; (P3) Terraform version drift (1.14.3 create vs 1.13.1 destroy → silent skip on state-version-mismatch). Apply-time error: azurerm_eventhub_namespace 'vpp-evh-premium-<slot>' already exists - needs to be imported into State. Fix is delete-recreate (when orphan is empty); destroy pipeline MUST verify zero residue before slot release.
root_cause: Destroy pipeline azure-pipeline-fbe-del.yml lacks residue-zero check; create/destroy pipelines on misaligned Terraform versions (F19).
fix: Add residue-zero check to destroy pipeline (per slot, query az resource list -g rg-vpp-app-sb-401 for resources matching slot name; fail if non-zero). Align Terraform versions between create (1.14.3) and destroy (1.13.1). Audit all 10 slots for existing orphans.

summary: Alerts created manually outside IaC during platform stand-up never get adopted into IaC; their KQL/thresholds age silently because no review surface owns them. Today's CMC alert rule was created 2024-01-24 by Conclusion vendor identity eelke.hoffman@conclusion.nl; single-predicate KQL; sev-0; autoMitigate=false; actions=null — escaped IaC review for 15.5 months until firing on Microsoft 5Z1B-6KG-driven late ingestion. Defense: quarterly inventory diff (az monitor scheduled-query list per subscription vs IaC-derived expected name list); any unmatched rule is adopt-or-delete within one sprint.
root_cause: No review surface; no quarterly audit cadence; vendor-identity alert authoring left no IaC trace.
fix: Quarterly inventory diff job (CI or scheduled task); alert-authoring checklist requires PR-only rule creation (no portal authoring on prd); CI gate refuses actions=null. Vendor-identity governance: should vendors create sev-0 paging rules on prd subscription? Surface to SRE/Platform leadership.

summary: SBSM (servicebus-subscriptions-manager) has THREE CD pipeline patterns, not the two its docs describe: (1) cd-myriad-platform (8574) auto-deploys on myriad-platform.yaml + infra/terragrunt/** changes; (2) cd-<consumer-team> per-contract auto-deploy pipelines exist for major consumers (e.g. cd-flex-trade-optimizer = 8995) and auto-trigger on their own contract yaml + infra/terragrunt/**/subscription_creator/** changes with NO approval gates; (3) cd-general (8573) is manual-trigger fallback for contracts WITHOUT a dedicated pipeline (used today only by asset-scheduling). Repo docs (docs/internals/cicd/cd-ado-pipeline-configuration.md) describe only patterns 1 and 3 — pattern 2 is undocumented and a likely source of incorrect on-call diagnoses. Pipeline-success != contract-realization: a Terragrunt apply --auto-approve can exit 0 while one of N for_each-keyed resources is silently absent from Azure; only an externally-witnessable parity probe (yaml entries vs Azure resources per env) proves full realization.
root_cause: Three independent issues: (a) docs/internals/cicd/cd-ado-pipeline-configuration.md is stale and lists only cd-myriad-platform and cd-general; (b) per-contract auto-deploy pipelines are registered via just ado-pipelines-register without doc updates; (c) Terragrunt apply --auto-approve --non-interactive can return success codes despite a single-resource silent skip whose root mechanism is uncharacterized (likely provider-level partial failure on parallel for_each resource creation).
fix: On-call triage for 'subscription missing on SBSM-managed contract': (1) az servicebus topic show — falsify topic-missing; (2) grep contract yaml for (topic_name, subscription_name, environment_name) tuple — falsify contract-missing; (3) az pipelines list --query for cd-<consumer-team> to identify the actual deployment pipeline (do NOT assume cd-general); (4) parity probe ALL subs for same consumer+env — 1/N missing localises to single-resource drift; 0/N localises to pipeline never ran; (5) MANDATORY gate-zero before any pipeline trigger: terragrunt state list against the affected env backend — state-has-resource branches to terragrunt state rm + re-apply (no-op apply trap), state-lacks-resource branches to pipeline re-trigger. References RCA at log/employer/eneco/02_on_call_shift/2026_05_12_topic_not_found/rca.md with full evidence chain.

summary: Adversarial subagent dispatch MUST include explicit 'Write your receipt to <absolute-path>, return only the file path' instruction; coordinator MUST run `test -s` on each artifact before consuming the agent's output. Brain rule NN-2 mandates that subagent artifacts are always written to disk. Brain rule 'Delegation discipline: >200 prescriptive tokens = agent-as-file-writer' applies to nearly all adversarial dispatches (Sherlock/Socrates/El-Demoledor prompts are typically 400-600 tokens). Returning prose in task notifications is NOT compliant — the receipt only lives in conversation history and is invisible to future sessions, audit, and lessons-learned harvesting.
root_cause: Tone-bypass — user framings like 'quick check first', 'just take a look', 'wdyt' relax coordinator discipline despite brain explicitly stating 'tone is signal, never exemption'. The relaxation manifests as: skipping manifest.json init, skipping P1 preflight mirror to disk, skipping artifact-write instructions in subagent prompts, skipping post-dispatch `test -s` verification. Real incident: 2026-05-12 FTO strike-price RCA had three adversarial reviewers (sherlock, socrates, el-demoledor) return prose to task notifications; zero files written by agents; coordinator detected the violation only when user asked directly. Receipts had to be reconstructed verbatim post-hoc into .ai/tasks/2026-05-12-001_topic-not-found/adversarial/*.md.
fix: Mandatory dispatch template for adversarial subagents: prompt MUST end with 'Write your full receipt as a markdown file to <absolute-path>/<reviewer-name>.md including frontmatter (task_id, agent, status, summary, date). Return ONLY the file path on completion. The coordinator will Read the file.' Coordinator post-dispatch MUST: (1) wait for completion notification; (2) run `test -s <path>` to verify non-empty file; (3) Read the file to consume the receipt; (4) update manifest.json adversarial_receipts[] with the path. Pre-flight rule: when emitting GATE-CLEARED, also create manifest.json with task_id/slug/task_root/external_writes/adversarial_receipts/gate_witnesses fields. The 'quick check first' / 'simple task' framing does NOT downgrade these mandates.

summary: Azure AD AADSTS650057 on `az` CLI against an Eneco custom API is structurally ambiguous: the error blames the client's app registration, but the load-bearing missing entity is almost always on the resource side or in a separate runtime directory object. Three gates control whether AAD will issue a delegated token: (1) `resource.api.preAuthorizedApplications` includes the client appId; (2) tenant-wide `oauth2PermissionGrant` (consentType=AllPrincipals) exists for client+resource+scope; (3) per-user `oauth2PermissionGrant` (consentType=Principal) exists for client+resource+scope+user. Group membership (`appRoleAssignedTo` for a group) and `appRoleAssignmentRequired` are DIFFERENT gates that affect what the token CONTAINS, not whether AAD issues one. Eneco BTM convention does not use `preAuthorizedApplications`, so new joiners hit (3)-only paths and fail until they consent once via `az login --scope api://<resource>/<scope>`.
root_cause: BTM platform-AAD convention relies on per-user consent rather than admin pre-authorization. Per-user consent grants are runtime directory objects invisible to Terraform; they drift independently of IaC. Each new joiner must complete the interactive consent step manually; no automation, no onboarding doc captures this. PR 172140 ('Fix not being able to get tokens from public clients (bruno)') added Bruno redirect URIs but did NOT add Azure CLI to preAuthorizedApplications, so anyone reading the commit assumes az CLI now works tenant-wide — it does not.
fix: Triage in three commands: (a) `az ad app show --id <api-guid> --query api.preAuthorizedApplications`; (b) `az rest GET /v1.0/oauth2PermissionGrants?$filter=clientId eq '<client-sp-id>' and resourceId eq '<resource-sp-id>' and consentType eq 'AllPrincipals'`; (c) same filter with `principalId eq '<user-oid>'`. The first non-empty result names the gate. If all empty, run `az login --scope api://<api-guid>/<scope>` interactively to create the per-user grant. Durable fix options: admin-grant tenant-wide consent (`az ad app permission grant --id <client> --api <resource> --scope <scope>`) or declare `pre_authorized_applications` in IaC (changes BTM convention; needs platform-AAD sign-off and security review).

summary: Azure Key Vault stores a secret VALUE as an opaque string. `az keyvault secret show --query value` with the DEFAULT/`-o json` output returns it JSON-escaped (wrapping quotes + literal \n on a single line), which looks like a corrupt/malformed PEM. `-o tsv` (or `az keyvault secret download --file`) returns the raw byte-clean multi-line PEM. The Azure Portal's single-line 'Secret value' field reproduces the same visual symptom. Therefore 'the PEM from Key Vault is not in good format' is almost always a READ-METHOD artifact, not corrupt storage. Confirm storage is fine: re-read with -o tsv, then openssl x509 -noout -subject. Validate a cert SET in 3 moves: parse+dates (openssl x509 -dates), chain verify (openssl verify -CAfile ca leaf), key<->cert public-half match (diff <(openssl pkey -in key -pubout) <(openssl x509 -in cert -noout -pubkey)).
root_cause: JSON output must escape newline (0x0A) as the two characters backslash-n and wrap strings in quotes; reading the JSON/Portal view and concluding the stored secret is broken confuses the read encoding with the storage.
fix: Re-read: az keyvault secret show --vault-name <kv> --name <k> --query value -o tsv > out.pem . Detect the artifact: grep -cF '\n' out.pem (0 = clean). Then verify with openssl as above.

summary: On-call requests to 'provide the Kafka certificate' (or any cert) frequently include a PRIVATE KEY (e.g. kafka-sslkey). Handing private keys over Slack/email copies them into durable, searchable, off-platform storage that cannot be reliably revoked — a secret-handling anti-pattern. The intended pattern is runtime fetch by the application via managed identity / RBAC; a human needing the raw key by hand signals local dev/test, a consumer not wired to KV, or missing KV access. Correct resolution = SELF-SERVE: grant the requester KV read RBAC + give them the correct `-o tsv` retrieval command, so the key never traverses chat. If a key has already been shared, ROTATE it. Minimum-exposure handoff if unavoidable = secret manager / 1Password share, never plain Slack/email, delete after use.
root_cause: Key Vault exists so secrets are NOT copied into uncontrolled systems; manual extraction + chat hand-off bypasses that boundary and creates an unrevocable leak.
fix: Redirect the request to self-serve KV access + retrieval command; flag rotation if the key was already shared; never paste private-key material into Slack, email, or the agent transcript. Also relevant: vpp-agg-* KVs are firewall-restricted per env (need MC SP login + IP whitelist via eneco-tools-connect-mc-environments, then whitelist-OFF); the Sandbox vault vpp-agg-sb is reachable directly.

summary: A pipeline step running `az boards`/`az repos` WITHOUT --organization/--project triggers the azure-devops CLI's git-remote context auto-detection: it reads `git config remote.origin.url` and calls `GET <org>/<project>/_git/<repo>/vsts/info` (cached in ~/.azure/azuredevops/cache/remotes.json). On an EPHEMERAL Microsoft-hosted agent the cache is ALWAYS cold, so the call fires every run. Under the project setting enforceJobAuthScope=true the project-scoped Build Service token (System.AccessToken) is denied that repo-context read -> `TF401019: The Git repository ... does not exist or you do not have permissions ... 404` (a 404-masks-403). The lowercased repo id in the error == the /vsts/info URL repo id, byte-for-byte. This makes a *Boards* command emit a *git-repository* error even though tagging never reads the repo. Switching the agent pool does NOT fix it: the auth identity is the project Build Service identity, INDEPENDENT of the pool (a pool swap only masks via a different az/extension version or a cached broad credential, and costs a second runner + job split).
root_cause: The CLI's implicit context auto-detection is a privileged repo-context read; under a scoped pipeline token it is denied. The script relied on auto-detection instead of explicit context. Onset is external (no code change): a long-static script breaks when enforceJobAuthScope is enabled or the hosted agent's az/azure-devops extension version starts performing the cold-cache /vsts/info detection.
fix: Pass explicit, detection-free context: `az boards query --organization "$SYSTEM_COLLECTIONURI" --project "$SYSTEM_TEAMPROJECT" --detect false`. The /vsts/info call then never happens (verified: `--debug | grep -c vsts/info` = 0). NOTE: `az boards work-item show/update` accept --org/--detect but REJECT --project (work items are org-global by id: `unrecognized arguments: --project`) -- use two contexts. Only `az boards query` needs the fix (work-item show/update never call /vsts/info). Do NOT 'switch to the Core Platform runner' as the fix. Triage: (1) `az repos show` proves the repo exists -> it's permission, not missing; (2) is the failing call an az boards/az repos call without --org? (3) `az devops invoke build generalSettings --query enforceJobAuthScope`.

summary: GREEN step != realized effect. A bash pipeline step with no `set -e` that runs a command inside a process substitution (`while read ...; do ...; done < <(cmd)`) SWALLOWS cmd's non-zero exit, so the ADO task succeeds while the work silently never happened (BTM: `az boards query` returned TF401019, the tag was never applied, build stayed green for ~6 weeks). Detection/fix: verify the REALIZED effect (the tag/resource/state), never the step status. Two more reusable moves from this incident: (a) DATE a silent onset by bisecting historical build logs for the error string -- `az devops invoke build builds maxTime=<date> ... resultFilter=succeeded` -> timeline -> the step's log -> grep; this turned 'weeks or months ago' into a 10-day window (2026-04-15 OK -> 04-25 broken) and, with no code change in that window, proved an external/org trigger. (b) For a COSMETIC post-deploy step (no continueOnError) make failures loud-but-NON-blocking via `echo ##vso[task.logissue type=error]` + `##vso[task.complete result=SucceededWithIssues]` -- a bare `set -euo pipefail` would convert a transient WIT 5xx or a future cross-project item into a deploy-blocking RED build.
root_cause: Process substitution runs in a child whose exit code does not propagate to the parent; without set -e the parent exits 0. The original also clobbered tags: a flat `az boards query` returns only System.Id (no System.Tags), so the old `read -r id tags` got tags='' and wrote `System.Tags=; TAG`, replacing the tag list -- harmless only because BtM items had no other tags.
fix: Capture command output into a variable (so set -e / explicit check can catch failure) instead of piping into a process substitution; assert the realized effect in verification; read-modify-write the UNION for additive fields (System.Tags), never overwrite; use SucceededWithIssues for non-blocking visibility on cosmetic steps. Onset-bisection one-liner template lives in the incident's rca.md L11.

summary: nginx ingress prefix-mount WITHOUT rewrite-target makes a HEALTHY app 404 its own routes. agg.dev telemetryfunctiontestsfn 404: ingress path /telemetryfunctiontestsfn/ (pathType Prefix, no rewrite) forwards the unstripped path to an Azure Functions host that serves /healthz at ROOT. siteregistry works ONLY because mounted at '/'. deliveryreportfn 404s identically (systemic). Topology: DNS->App Gateway vpp-agw-d (WAF_v2, Basic pass-through, 20.76.210.221)->AKS nginx v1.14.0 (50.85.91.121, ns vpp-agg)->ClusterIP:8080->pod.
root_cause: AGIC->nginx migration (PR 150758, Nov-2025) switched ingress class to nginx and dropped the appgw annotation, but never added the nginx equivalent. The chart's only prefix-strip was appgw.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-path-prefix (AGIC), which nginx silently ignores. nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target was never committed (git -S = 0). Legacy migration gap, not a regression. Verified vs live helm release v215 chart 0.1.27 (annotations:{}).
fix: Add to the chart values (Eneco.Vpp.Aggregation/azure-pipeline/Helm/<svc>/values.yaml; mirror in deliveryreportfn): path /<svc>(/|$)(.*) + nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex:'true' + nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target:/$2 (class already nginx). Proven by composition (backend /healthz=200). Restores /healthz only; the *fn are timer/Kafka-triggered QA test functions (ADR AL006), no HTTP functions (/api/*=404,/admin/*=401). Alternative: use canonical OpenShift agg.dev-mc (Route rewrite already correct) but it is internal-only (needs AVD whitelist, wiki 44740). NOT a fix: image re-sync or AVD whitelist.

summary: Triage heuristic for 'X not accessible from AVD': a clean HTTP 404 over a working TCP/TLS connect means the edge is UP and ROUTING -> it is a PATH problem, NOT network. Do not chase AVD whitelist/VNET/Private Endpoint on a 404 (those give timeout/403). Bisect ingress vs backend with `kubectl -n <ns> port-forward svc/<svc> 8080:8080`: if backend /healthz=200 but /<prefix>/healthz=404, the bug is the ingress prefix/rewrite, not the app. A service mounted at '/' is the false-negative control that hides prefix-rewrite bugs across siblings. Also: prove origin with the nginx access log (upstream IP + status), not header heuristics (Server header may be absent even on 200s). And verify deploy-state from the LIVE helm release (decode secret sh.helm.release.v1.<rel>.vN: data.release | base64 -d | base64 -d | gunzip | jq) rather than stale local clones.
root_cause: AVD framing biases toward a network hypothesis; the status code + a port-forward bisection localize the real layer in two probes.
fix: Apply the 404-over-clean-connect heuristic; bisect with port-forward; cite access log for origin; decode live helm release for deployed values.

summary: VPP Aggregation Layer Kafka (ESP/Axual) mTLS CLIENT certs (CN esp-eet-vpp-{dt,acc,prd}.streaming.eneco.com) are issued as a password-protected PFX by Networking4All (Jenke van Gerven) front-ending the Trust Provider B.V./DigiCert PKI, requested via ServiceNow, ~1yr validity, MANUAL (no ACME). The PFX is split with openssl into kafka-cacert/kafka-clientcert/kafka-sslkey + keystore password. OWNER: Fabrizio Zavalloni (operator who runs esp-eet-vpp-* renewals) + Roel van de Grint (Axual apps + Networking4All contact). RUNBOOK: wiki 'Myriad - Aggregation Layer / Runbook certificate rotation aggregation layer' (Page 50903) via ArgoCD app esp-certificate-agg -> recreate 'keys' k8s secret -> update PFX password in keyvault -> restart. MONITORING BLIND SPOT: the #expiring-certificates Slack bot AND the ESP expiry pipeline (definitionId 2735 -> myriad-alerts-devops) only list NOT-YET-expired certs; once a cert expires it disappears from the digest, so an already-expired cert is invisible. No documented sandbox->runtime propagation; runbook is prod-centric. RECURRENCE: near-identical agg-layer expiry 2025-09-08 (same KVs vpp-agg-sb / vpp-agg-appsec-{d,a,p}).
root_cause: Manual reseller-issued PFX + prod-only runbook + expiry monitoring that hides already-expired certs + sandbox-vault (vpp-agg-sb) vs runtime-vault (vpp-agg-appsec-{d,a,p}) divergence: a 2026-05-29 rotation landed only in sandbox and was never propagated to runtime, so dev (esp-eet-vpp-dt) and acc (esp-eet-vpp-acc) certs both stayed EXPIRED from 2026-01-10.
fix: On agg-layer Kafka cert expiry: (1) align with Fabrizio first (a company-wide ESP rotation may already be in flight - Dec-2025 effort); (2) follow runbook 50903 per environment; (3) request re-issue via Networking4All/ServiceNow if no valid PFX exists (acc had none); (4) ALWAYS verify the RUNTIME vault vpp-agg-appsec-{d,a,p}, not just sandbox vpp-agg-sb; (5) add an ALREADY-EXPIRED-aware expiry alarm (the current digest only catches not-yet-expired). LL-006 class.

summary: A K8s Secret present in some environments but missing in ONE (pod FailedMount "secret X not found") is usually a DEPLOY-PATH ENROLLMENT GAP, not dead code. Different envs can use different deploy paths: MC may deploy a Helm chart via a GitOps app-of-apps + OCI registry, while a sandbox/legacy env uses an in-repo pipeline that omits it. A single repo string-search can FALSE-NEGATIVE (glob push-loops like `for i in $(ls Helm/)` never contain the literal chart path) - verify via CONSUMERS (OCI registry, GitOps repo). Use ownerReferences (NOT managedFields) as the manual-vs-controller discriminator: on K8s 1.31.x even CSI-projected secrets can have empty managedFields. CSI SecretProviderClass secretObjects are projected only while a pod mounts the SPC as a CSI volume (mount-coupled, can orphan when an unrelated mounting pod scales to 0); ESO is mount-independent.
root_cause: Investigations stop at "where is X defined?" (grep) instead of "what deploys X in THIS environment?". Env-specific deploy paths diverge (GitOps+OCI vs legacy in-repo Helm). managedFields can be empty for controller-projected secrets, making it an invalid provenance signal. CSI projected secrets are a side-effect of an active SPC CSI-volume mount, not standalone objects.
fix: Triage "secret in env B but missing in env A": (1) kubectl get secret X -o json -> ownerReferences absent + no helm/csi labels => hand-made; (2) check BOTH deploy paths incl. the GitOps repo + OCI registry, never trust one ado-repo-search; (3) if wiring via CSI, the CONSUMING pods MUST mount the SPC CSI volume or the projection orphans - prefer ESO; (4) NEVER delete a shared chart/template another environment still renders from (verify cross-env consumption first). Full chain: task 2026-06-02-001 RCA.

summary: FBE 'recreation succeeded but only app-of-apps + 1 service' is an ArgoCD finalizer deadlock, not a branch/render bug. The slot's Applications are stuck Terminating (deletionTimestamp + resources-finalizer); ArgoCD logs 'Skipping auto-sync: deletion in progress' and the owning ApplicationSet cannot recreate a same-named app-of-apps.
root_cause: A chronically failing PostSync seed hook (alarmengine POST /v1/assets/seed -> HTTP 500) leaves its Job with argocd.argoproj.io/hook-finalizer; on a later delete the cascade wedges on that Job -> child Application can't delete -> app-of-apps can't delete -> ApplicationSet can't recreate. Symptom: only the leftover app-of-apps + the wedged child show; ApplicationSet conditions are all green (render/generation is fine), which DISPROVES the developer's usual 'I broke my branch's vpp-config' render hypothesis but NOT branch runtime-deployability. RESOLVED 2026-06-04: after clearing the deadlock, the FBE auto-rebuilt and alarmengine seed returned 204 (not 500) with secrets present -> the seed-500 was the missing secret-provider-kv (a deadlock symptom: secretprovider child wasn't deployed), NOT the developer's branch content. Branch exonerated by live evidence. Lesson: the chronically-failing seed that wedged the finalizer was itself downstream of the half-deployed state, so a clean recreate fixes both.
fix: Diagnose: kubectl -n argocd logs argocd-application-controller-0 | grep <slot> | grep 'deletion in progress'; check .metadata.deletionTimestamp + finalizers. Unblock (Tier-1, voltex-scoped, literal names only): strip the stuck hook Job finalizer (kubectl -n <slot> patch job <seed-job> --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":[]}}'), force-delete zombie pods, watch 'objects remaining' drain; ApplicationSet auto-recreates (no pipeline rerun). Last resort gated: strip resources-finalizer child-then-parent. THEN Track 2: clearing the deadlock is necessary-not-sufficient -- capture the alarmengine log at seed time to pin the 500 (branch image/appconfig vs missing secret-provider-kv SecretProviderClass) or it recurs. NEVER: destroy pipeline 2629 as rollback; argocd app delete --cascade (can't reach the k8s Job finalizer); -l/--all/wrong-ns; touching <slot>-monitoring.

summary: Cosmos NormalizedRUConsumption is a per-partition-key-range MAX utilization GAUGE (0-100%, 1-min), NOT a client-impact metric. Confirm throttling with the HTTP-429 RATE = TotalRequests(StatusCode=429) / TotalRequests(all), per Microsoft's 1-5%-healthy / >5%-act framework. Do NOT use the gauge % or the Mongo error-16500 COUNT as the impact figure: 16500 is counted per protocol-op INCLUDING driver retries (up to ~9x), so it is inflated. STAKES-CLAIM: if on-call/RCA acts on the gauge or the 16500 count instead of the 429-rate, they over-provision RU or page on healthy-band noise (or, inversely, dismiss a real >5% throttle). 2026-06-15 gurobi-cosmos: 77.67% RU-avg + 16,555 16500-events implied 'severe ~34.5% throttle', but the true HTTP-429 rate was 586/20792 = 2.82% — INSIDE Microsoft's 1-5% healthy band. FALSIFIER: a window where 429-rate>5% was treated as noise, or where 16500-count equalled distinct-op failures (no retries) — either would break the rate-not-count rule. STAKES-CLASS: HIGH.
root_cause: NormalizedRUConsumption = max over partition-key-ranges per minute; a PT15M Average of per-minute maxes can be brief sub-second spikes (confirm with TotalRequestUnits: the 2026-06-15 hot minutes served only ~3292 RU/min ~= 55 RU/s avg). Mongo 16500 counts every rejected protocol op incl. SDK retries, so its count >> distinct throttled ops and >> the backend HTTP-429 count (16,555 vs 586).
fix: Triage a NormalizedRU alert with: az monitor metrics list --metric TotalRequests --filter "StatusCode eq '429'" (numerator) over the same metric all-status (denominator) => rate. <=5% + self-resolved = healthy/ack; >5% or sustained = act/escalate. Size autoscale from TotalRequestUnits demand, not the gauge. For spiky workloads, page on 429-rate>5% and demote the RU% gauge to a Sev3 warning.

summary: cosmosdb-gurobi-platform-a (rg-gurobi-platform-a, Mongo API, db grb_rsm) backs the Gurobi Cluster Manager. Its HOT collection is the unsharded GridFS fs.chunks at autoscale max 1000 RU/s; FleetOptimizer batch solves micro-burst it to 100% multiple times daily (chronic). The team (PR 176135, commit f956e9b) REMOVED the 429-count throttling alert and added a leading NormalizedRU>75%/PT15M/Sev2 gauge => there is now NO direct client-impact (429) page. STAKES-CLAIM: blind-ack misses a real >5% throttle; blind-page floods on-call on healthy-band micro-bursts. Triage routing: 429-RATE<=5% + self-resolved (autoMitigate) => ack + link the RCA; 429-rate>5% OR sustained across windows OR Gurobi solves failing => escalate to Nuno (fallback #team-platform). Throughput is NOT Terraform-managed (Cluster Manager app creates collections). FALSIFIER: prod -p live throughput differs from acc's 1000 RU/s (prod may be portal-bumped higher), or fs.chunks gets sharded / GridFS blobs moved to Blob Storage. STAKES-CLASS: HIGH.
root_cause: Single-partition GridFS large-object I/O (input models/solutions) at a 1000 RU/s ceiling saturates during bursty parallel solves; chronic, mildly under-provisioned. The alert is a conservative leading gauge not tuned for a spiky workload, with no companion client-impact signal.
fix: (T2/primary) re-add a 429-RATE page (>5%, per-env backtested) + demote RU gauge to Sev3 + fix its stale 'for more than 5 minutes' description (window is PT15M). (T1/secondary) raise fs.chunks autoscale max 1000->~4000 (single partition serves up to 10k RU/s, no shard needed; instantMax=10000) and bring throughput into IaC. (T3/candidate, feasibility A3) offload GridFS large objects to Blob Storage or shard fs.chunks — depends on the third-party Gurobi Cluster Manager supporting it. Verify prod separately.

summary: git pull (fetch latest) before trusting an IaC NEGATIVE ('X is not in code'). A stale local clone flips provenance conclusions. STAKES-CLAIM: an 'X not in IaC' read from a stale clone routes the fix to the wrong place (portal/CCoE vs code). 2026-06-15: a Jan-27 gurobi-infrastructure clone (predating the 2026-03-27 RCA) said the NormalizedRU alert was not in IaC => 'externally provisioned'; pulling to the Jun-05 HEAD reversed it — the alert is team-authored (src/locals.tf) and the team had deliberately added it. For Eneco ADO repos the ssh key is loaded via the 'sshwork' alias (ssh-add ~/.ssh/work/eneco/eneco_personal); the MC acc SP login is 'enecotfvppmcloginacc' (1Password), cleared with 'enecotflogout'. FALSIFIER: a case where the freshly-pulled clone STILL lacks the artifact and it genuinely is portal/out-of-IaC. STAKES-CLASS: MEDIUM.
root_cause: Investigations read whatever HEAD the local clone happens to be at; a months-stale clone is a silent context gap that an evidence-labeled A1 claim ('not in IaC') hides.
fix: Before any 'not in IaC' / provenance claim: sshwork && git -C <repo> fetch --all && git pull --ff-only && git log -1; cross-check live Azure (az monitor metrics alert show / resource tags) which is more authoritative than the repo for what is deployed now.

summary: Azure App Configuration access failures on MC dev split cleanly by HTTP status AND caller. STATUS: 401 = authentication failed (no/invalid/expired/wrong-tenant Entra token, or a deleted/stale/mis-signed access key); 403 = authorization (missing App Configuration Data role) OR network (application/problem+json type ip-address-rejected / nsp-rejected); timeout = off-VNet. These are mutually exclusive: a missing role is 403 not 401; a blocked VPN is 403/timeout not 401. CALLER: 'the FFs cannot be set' hides THREE callers with three credentials — (i) interactive user from AVD (your own Entra token), (ii) the FF-apply ADO Terraform pipeline SP eneco-vpp-mc-dev (its Entra token; needs App Configuration Data Owner, granted via group sg-vpp-core-release-masters), (iii) the running FBE/VPP pod reading flags (an access-key connection string from KV secret connectionstrings-app-config via Eneco.Vpp.Configuration.AzureAppConfiguration .Connect(connectionString)). Same status from a different caller = different fix. 'I can see the flags in the portal' is control-plane (ARM Reader) visibility, NOT proof the data-plane call authenticates (and the portal blade can itself 401 on a private-endpoint store in Edge). The store is public_network_access=Disabled + private-endpoint-only ('everything through AVD'); never open public access. Strongest base-rate cause for 'FFs not set' on a slot = the manual approval-gated App Config pipeline never ran/approved (same filer Duncan, FBE Kidu, 2025-09), which predicts NO real 401.
root_cause: Two distinct App Config data-plane code paths (access-key connection-string READ; Entra-SP WRITE via the ADO Terraform pipeline) plus three possible callers plus a control-plane portal view independent of both. Conflating the paths/callers, or treating a rusty filer's paraphrased '401' as a captured status line, routes the fix to the wrong layer. The 18-Jun VPN-timeout ticket (resolved by-design: private endpoint, use AVD) is a DIFFERENT layer from a 401-on-AVD.
fix: Capture the EXACT status + WWW-Authenticate/problem+json body + WHICH caller before acting (it is AVD-gated). 401 Bearer (you) -> az login refresh to the dev-mc tenant; 401 Bearer (pipeline SP) -> rotate the eneco-vpp-mc-dev ADO service-connection secret (NOT a personal login); 401 HMAC (FBE pod) -> check live disableLocalAuth, then re-run the App Config IaC pipeline so Terraform rewrites connectionstrings-app-config (a manual 'az keyvault secret set' on that IaC-managed secret DRIFTS and is silently reverted by the next apply; if unavoidable use a READ key, non-empty guard, --file/stdin not --value, capture prior value); 403 no body -> grant least data role: a DIRECT App Configuration Data Reader for a read, Data Owner via the IaC group ONLY for the apply SP (never add a reader to sg-vpp-core-release-masters — it is store-wide Data Owner + release-master authority); 403 problem+json -> use AVD / link private DNS, never enable public network access; no error -> approve+run the FF pipeline. 'az role assignment list' is a control-plane read needing ARM Reader — a command error there is not 'no role'. Verify by EFFECT (failing op returns 200 / slot reads flag), never exit code or portal view. Member of the LL-006 credential/access class.

summary: agg.<env>.vpp.eneco.com '<svc>fn/...' 404 from AVD = nginx ingress PREFIX mount with NO rewrite-target, NOT a network/AVD/whitelist problem. Triage by status shape: a clean 404 over a completed TLS handshake + a working SIBLING path (e.g. /api/siteregistry=200) => path routing, not network (network gives timeout/refused; WAF gives 403). The *fn test functions are prefix-mounted (/telemetryfunctiontestsfn/) but the Azure-Functions backend serves /healthz at ROOT, so the unstripped prefixed path 404s; siteregistry works only because it is mounted at /. Born from the AGIC->nginx migration (appgw backend-path-prefix dropped, nginx rewrite-target never added). App Gateway vpp-agw-d is pass-through (urlPathMaps:[]). This RECURRED 2026-06-22 (telemetryfunctiontestsfn, blocking Anasthasia's release e2e) because the 2026-06-02 fix was never merged: canonical chart on ADO default branch 'development' still has annotations:{} after ~15 redeploys (release v215->v230). A diagnosed fix in an RCA file is not a fix until merged+deployed.
root_cause: Reverse-proxy prefix mount without a path rewrite: the backend has no PathBase for the mount prefix, so the proxy must strip it. nginx-ingress rewrite-target was never added when dev migrated from AGIC to standalone nginx. Recurrence cause is organizational: the documented fix was never landed into the Helm chart, so every redeploy re-rendered the broken ingress.
fix: TWO-file Helm chart change in Eneco.Vpp.Aggregation (azure-pipeline/Helm/<svc>/): (1) values.yaml -> path: /<svc>(/|$)(.*) + annotations nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex:'true' and rewrite-target:/$2 ; (2) templates/ingress.yaml -> pathType: Prefix => ImplementationSpecific. pathType change is MANDATORY: nginx admission webhook REJECTS a regex path under pathType:Prefix ('cannot be used with pathType Prefix') - proven live. use-regex does NOT rescue Prefix. Verify by EFFECT: edge curl /<svc>/healthz=200 AND /api/siteregistry stays 200 AND decoded live Helm release ingress.annotations != {} (else you kubectl-edited and it will drift). Immediate unblock: kubectl -n vpp-agg port-forward svc/<svc> 8080:8080 then curl localhost:8080/healthz=200 (needs AKS vpp-aks01-d access AVD has). Do NOT kubectl edit the managed ingress (Helm-managed, overwritten next deploy). Do NOT chase whitelist/VNET/PE/new-image. NOTE per-fn health path differs: telemetryfunctiontestsfn serves /healthz=200 at root; deliveryreportfn serves /=200 but /healthz=404 (use /<svc>/ for its liveness). agg.dev-mc (OpenShift Route) already has the correct rewrite but is internal-only (NXDOMAIN public; needs AVD whitelist).
stakes_claim: If a responder treats 'agg *fn not visible from AVD' as a network/whitelist issue, the fix targets the wrong layer and the release stays blocked; the real fix is the two-file ingress rewrite with pathType ImplementationSpecific.
falsifier: A prefixed *fn path returning 200 with no rewrite-target present, OR a values-only rewrite (pathType:Prefix) being accepted by the admission webhook. Neither occurred (proven evidence/02, evidence/09).

summary: An Azure Key Vault certificate is a composite of cert+key+SECRET sharing one name. If a certificate is imported (e.g. by hand) with the same name as a Terraform-managed azurerm_key_vault_secret, the cert's backing secret 'captures' it and `terraform destroy` fails with 403 SecretManagedByKeyVault ('delete the corresponding certificate'). FBE thor stuck-deletion run 1.
root_cause: Importing a KV certificate auto-creates a managed secret + key of the same name. Terraform can DeleteSecret a plain secret but not a certificate-owned (managed=true) one. The collision is invisible to terraform plan because the cert is not in state.
fix: Delete the CERTIFICATE (az keyvault certificate delete), not the secret — this removes the backing secret. Verify `az keyvault certificate list`=[] and `az keyvault secret list --query "[?managed]"`=[]; then the 403 class cannot recur and destroy proceeds (404-on-refresh on the absent secret is graceful).
stakes_claim: If a responder deletes the colliding SECRET (or re-runs destroy) to clear the 403, it cannot work — only deleting the CERTIFICATE removes the managed backing secret.
falsifier: A successful `az keyvault secret delete` on a managed (cert-backed) secret, or destroy succeeding while a same-named certificate still exists. Neither is possible per Azure KV semantics; live probe confirmed cert+secret both gone after the cert was deleted.

summary: The FBE 'Feature Branch Environment - Delete' pipeline is non-idempotent. After a partial teardown a re-run fails EARLIER: Preparation resolves a now-deleted resource name to empty (az appconfig list contains(name,'<slot>')=>'') and DestroyAppConfiguration runs `az appconfig feature list -n ''` => 'argument --name/-n: expected one argument' before reaching DestroyInfra. The slot stays 'used' because 'Release environment' is the last step of DestroyInfra.
root_cause: Resolve-then-use stages assume the resource still exists. 'Stages to run -> DestroyInfra only' also fails: DestroyInfra has no explicit dependsOn + no stage condition, so ADO's default stage succeeded() cascade-skips it when the deselected predecessor is Skipped.
fix: Guard DestroyAppConfiguration to succeed-noop when appconfig is empty (job-level condition ne(variables['appconfig'],'') via the existing stageDependencies var, or a bash exit 0); give DestroyInfra explicit dependsOn + condition in(dependencies.DestroyAppConfiguration.result,'Succeeded','Skipped'); then full re-run. Witness success by featurebranchenvdetails row active='unused', NOT a green build. Verify empty appconfig tfstate (~184B) before relying on the skip.
stakes_claim: If a responder 'just re-runs' the delete pipeline (whole, or DestroyInfra-only), the slot stays stuck: whole-run fails again at DestroyAppConfiguration; DestroyInfra-only silently cascade-skips.
falsifier: A whole-pipeline re-run completing past DestroyAppConfiguration with the store absent, OR a DestroyInfra-only run actually executing terraform destroy with the predecessor deselected. Build 1683370 + ADO stage semantics show neither happens.

summary: An ADO request to 'grant me edit rights for Project Settings -> Notifications' to get build-completion emails is usually an XY problem. ADO notifications have THREE scopes with different edit-permission: PERSONAL (User settings -> Notifications) needs NO elevated rights (role 'User'/project member) and delivers to the requester's own inbox; TEAM-level shared subs are editable by a Team Administrator; PROJECT-level shared subs require Project Administrators (there is NO narrow 'edit notifications' toggle -- the grant == Project Admin membership). Default resolution: tell the filer to self-serve a personal subscription (zero grant); grant Team Administrator (least-privilege, NOT Project Admin) only if a shared/DL recipient is genuinely needed. Event type = ms.vss-build.build-completed-event (legacy '...-legacy-event' variants reject custom subs). No native 'az devops notification' command -> use 'az rest' POST _apis/notification/subscriptions?api-version=7.1 with audience GUID 499b84ac-1321-427f-aa17-267ca6975798; resolve project id via 'az rest .../_apis/projects/<name>' (az login token, no extension/PAT -- 'az devops project show' needs the azure-devops extension's separate auth). The pipeline-id filter clause is undocumented -- build it in the portal and GET the sub back to copy criteria.clauses; never guess it.
root_cause: ADO surfaces the shared (admin) Notifications page prominently, so requesters assume it is the only door and ask for project-level edit rights (= Project Administrators); the personal self-serve route is less discoverable. The required permission is a consequence of the subscriber/audience, not a prerequisite.
fix: Triage with one question: 'who receives the email -- just you, or a team/shared list?' Just-you => personal sub, no grant (User settings -> Notifications -> New subscription -> Build -> 'A build completes', scope project, filter Build pipeline). Team => grant Team Administrator + shared sub. Project-wide => Project Administrators (last resort). Close on the RECEIVED email, never the HTTP 201 / green save (a sub with a dead filter, or a mailbox-less caller identity such as a service principal, returns 201 and delivers nothing). Full how-to + runbook in references.
stakes_claim: If on-call grants the literally-requested 'edit Project Settings -> Notifications' rights for a personal-email need, they grant Project Administrators membership (over-privilege) to solve a zero-permission self-serve task; the least-privilege grant for a genuinely shared sub is Team Administrator.
falsifier: An ADO tenant where personal notification subscriptions are disabled org-wide (so the personal route is unavailable), or where a narrow 'edit notifications' permission exists separate from Project Administrators. Microsoft Learn permission tables (notifications/about-notifications#notification-types) show neither for current azure-devops.

summary: In an Azure DevOps pipeline, setting env AZURE_DEVOPS_EXT_PAT=$(System.AccessToken) does NOT guarantee `az boards`/`az repos` run as the Build Service identity. If an earlier step did `az login` (e.g. azure-login.yml service-principal login for Terraform), that az login session takes PRECEDENCE over AZURE_DEVOPS_EXT_PAT, so the boards/repos call runs as the SP. BTM tagging failed because it ran as mcc-btm-deployment-dta-sp (no board read) not the Build Service. Confirmed: invalid-PAT local test still succeeds via az login; pipeline --debug shows ServicePrincipalCredential.acquire_token (ext 1.0.4).
root_cause: azure-devops CLI credential resolution prefers an existing az login session over the PAT env var; a job that logs in as an SP hijacks the identity of all subsequent az boards/az repos calls in that job.
fix: Run the boards/repos step in its OWN job WITHOUT the az login step (so only AZURE_DEVOPS_EXT_PAT/System.AccessToken is present -> Build Service identity), keeping the same MS-hosted pool. Alt: `az logout` before the call. A separate-job fix on the same pool needs no extra runner. (This is why sibling PR 178802's 'pool switch' worked: the new job dropped azure-login.yml = identity change, not pool.)

summary: Two read-only ADO permission tools LIE about a SUBJECT's effective permission. `az devops security permission show` -> resolvedPermissions[].effectivePermission reports 'Not set' for SERVICE/GROUP descriptors even when they DO have the permission via inheritance (reported the project Build Service as 'Not set' for View work items despite effAllow=241). `POST _apis/security/permissionevaluationbatch` IGNORES the subject descriptor and evaluates the CALLER (a garbage descriptor returns true). Trusting either can flip a diagnosis.
root_cause: permission show does not resolve inherited group grants for non-user identities; permissionevaluationbatch evaluates the authenticated caller, not the passed subject.
fix: Use GET _apis/accesscontrollists/{namespaceId}?token=<token>&includeExtendedInfo=true and read acesDictionary[descriptor].extendedInfo.effectiveAllow (a bitmask). For CSS (area path) namespace 83e28ad4-2d72-4ceb-97b0-c7726d5502c3: bit16=View work items, bit32=Edit work items. Discriminator proof: a user with effAllow=3 shows ViewWI=False; Build Service effAllow=241 shows ViewWI=True.

summary: Destructive `az` ops on this machine MUST pin `--subscription <id>` ON THE COMMAND ITSELF — the az CLI default (shared ~/.azure/azureProfile.json) can be flipped mid-task by a CONCURRENT session's `az account set`, and ARM_* env vars from the MC login do NOT govern az CLI resolution. 2026-06-26 deleting acc Cosmos collection AssetMonitor_FlexReservation: the read-only probe ran on acc (b524d084) but by the delete the default had drifted to Sandbox (7b1ba02e); the unpinned `az cosmosdb mongodb collection delete` targeted Sandbox and returned AuthorizationFailed (acc SP has no Sandbox rights) — a SAFE no-op but a wrong-target near-miss. TELL: an AuthorizationFailed whose error SCOPE subscription != your target is DRIFT, not a permissions gap. Cosmos collection delete is ARM control-plane (management.azure.com), governed by RBAC — it works WITHOUT the bootstrap-SA IP whitelist and is NOT blocked by a private-endpoint-only account. ALSO: before deleting to 'unblock' an IaC apply, check for an existing NON-destructive team fix — here a 2026-06-25 PR adopts the same collection via moves.tf (mongodb_collection -> mongodb_collection_shared_throughput) + use_shared_throughput=true (0 destroy); the '404/already exists' was a throughput-model mismatch (shared-db-throughput collection vs Terraform-declared collection-level autoscale). Surface the divergence; don't silently delete.
root_cause: az CLI subscription context is process-external shared state (azureProfile.json) mutable by any concurrent session; sre-safety-preflight.sh passing in one Bash batch does NOT hold into the next. Deleting the live collection unblocks the apply but loses data and (with current config) recreates it with the wrong dedicated throughput; the PR's adopt-via-moves.tf is the durable fix.
fix: For any destructive az op: (1) re-run `az account show --query id` AND pass explicit `--subscription <target>` on the destructive command (never rely on a default set in an earlier batch); (2) if the AuthorizationFailed scope subscription != target, it's DRIFT — `az account set` + re-pin, do not assume missing RBAC; (3) Cosmos collection delete = `az cosmosdb mongodb collection delete --subscription <id> --account-name <acct> -g <rg> --database-name <db> --name <coll> --yes` (control-plane, no whitelist); (4) witness NotFound + siblings before==after (H-EFFECT-1); (5) before deleting to unblock an apply, grep the repo for an existing moves.tf/import fix.
stakes_claim: If a responder runs a destructive az op relying on the default subscription (set earlier or by the MC login) instead of pinning --subscription, a concurrent session's az account set can silently retarget it at the WRONG subscription; here only the target SP's lack of rights in Sandbox prevented a wrong-env deletion.
falsifier: An `az cosmosdb mongodb collection delete` with no --subscription provably honoring the intended sub while azureProfile.json default points elsewhere, OR the same delete being blocked by the Cosmos account's private-endpoint/IP-whitelist. Neither occurred: the unpinned call hit Sandbox; the pinned call succeeded on acc.

summary: An FBE 'feature-flag 401' is almost never the dev-mc shared App Config store. Each FBE slot reads its OWN per-slot Sandbox store vpp-appconfig-fbe-<slot>-<rand>.azconfig.io (RG rg-vpp-app-sb-401), and the VPP frontend init container injects that store's access-key CONNECTION STRING into a browser-served file (window.VUE_APP_AZ_CONFIG_CONNECTION_STRING in /etc/nginx/html/appconfig/appconfig.js). So the failing 'app configuration calls' are the BROWSER SPA calling .appconfig.featureflag%2F* directly over HMAC, visible in DevTools. 'I can see the FFs set properly in the app config' usually means looking at the WRONG store (the dev-mc vpp-applicationconfig-d from an earlier ticket). For Jupiter (Rec0BC1FTLV35) the store/key were healthy throughout; the 401 was a provisioning-window freshness condition (store created 09:29Z, flags 10:03Z, Duncan tested ~10:49Z against a 23-min-old frontend pod, healthy pod rebuilt 20:17Z) that self-resolved — Duncan confirmed 401->200.
root_cause: Per-slot FBE App Config store + browser-direct HMAC read of feature flags; transient credential/appconfig.js freshness during FBE provisioning. The dev-mc vpp-applicationconfig-d framing is a misdirection inherited from the earlier (by-design) VPN-network ticket.
fix: Probe the LIVE FBE, not dev-mc. kubectl ctx vpp-aks01-d (Sandbox): endpoint = `kubectl -n <slot> get secret application-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.connectionstrings_appconfig}' | base64 -d | grep -oiE 'Endpoint=[^;]+'`. Validate the key: `CS=...; az appconfig kv list --connection-string "$CS" --top 1` (200 = key valid -> not a key/RBAC problem). Have the user hard-refresh and capture the DevTools request (host=which store, Authorization Id=, status, Date). Provisioning-window 401s self-resolve on the frontend rebuild; do NOT touch dev-mc keys/RBAC/pipeline. Incidental: the connection string (Primary write key) is exposed to the browser - separate hardening follow-up.
stakes_claim: If the next on-call routes an 'FBE feature-flag 401' to the dev-mc vpp-applicationconfig-d store (chasing keys/RBAC/the FF-apply pipeline), they fix the wrong resource: the real caller is the browser hitting the per-slot Sandbox store, and the fault is usually transient provisioning freshness that self-heals.
falsifier: An FBE whose frontend appconfig.js Endpoint= points at vpp-applicationconfig-d rather than a per-slot vpp-appconfig-fbe-<slot> store, OR a persistent FBE 401 where `az appconfig kv list --connection-string` against the FBE's own store also fails. Neither held for Jupiter: endpoint was vpp-appconfig-fbe-jupiter-qvc and the connection string returned 200 + real flags.
